hero.title: Words that move people, and move the needle.
hero.subtitle: I'm Neha Mishra — a content writer & copywriter crafting editorial stories, SEO-driven articles, and brand copy that earns attention. Based between Blacktown, Australia and Butwal, Nepal.

about.headline: A writer who treats words like architecture.
bio: I believe the right sentence can change a mind, launch a product, or quietly keep a reader up past midnight. For seven years I've written across continents — from the café-lined streets of Blacktown to the foothills of Butwal — translating complex ideas into prose that feels effortless.

My work sits at the intersection of craft and strategy. I write to be read, but I also write to rank, to convert, and to last. Whether it's a 2,000-word thought-leadership piece or a single line of brand copy, I bring the same obsessive care to the page.
about.philosophy: Good writing is invisible. The reader should feel the idea, not the effort behind it. I edit until the words disappear and only the meaning remains.

experience.description: Lead long-form content and SEO strategy for B2B and lifestyle clients across APAC.
Owned brand voice and conversion copy for hospitality and retail brands.
Ghostwritten articles, newsletters, and web copy for founders and publications.

services.title: Content Strategy
services.description: Editorial calendars, audience research, and a measurable plan that turns scattered ideas into a publishing engine.

services.title: SEO Writing
services.description: Keyword-led articles and landing pages that rank without reading like they were written by a robot.

services.title: Brand Copy
services.description: Taglines, manifests, and website copy with a voice people recognise and remember.

services.title: Social Media Content
services.description: Scroll-stopping captions, threads, and series that build a community, not just a following.

services.title: Technical Writing
services.description: Documentation, whitepapers, and explainers that make the complicated feel obvious.

services.title: Editorial & Ghostwriting
services.description: Thought-leadership articles written in your voice, published under your name.

portfolio.title: The Quiet Power of a Slow Morning
portfolio.excerpt: An essay on ritual, attention, and why the best ideas arrive before the inbox does.
portfolio.content:
There is a particular kind of silence that only exists before the world wakes. I have come to protect it the way others protect a meeting — with intention, with boundaries, with a small and stubborn refusal to be interrupted.

The slow morning is not laziness. It is a posture. It is the decision to meet the day before the day meets you, to let a single thought unfurl without the fever of a notification.

Writers talk about discipline, and they should. But discipline without rest is just friction. The quiet power of a slow morning is that it returns you to yourself, and from that self, the sentences finally come.

portfolio.title: How Small Brands Win with Search in 2025
portfolio.excerpt: A practical field guide to owning niche queries when you can't outspend the giants.
portfolio.content:
Every founder I meet asks the same question: how do we compete when the incumbents have million-dollar budgets? The answer is almost never 'spend more'. It is 'be specific'.

Search has shifted from keywords to intent. The queries that convert are long, weird, and human. They sound like the way a customer actually talks to a friend. Small brands win by living inside those queries the giants consider too small to chase.

Build content around the questions your customers ask at 11pm. Answer them better than anyone. Repeat. That is the whole strategy, and it still works.

portfolio.title: Introducing Lumen: Copy That Feels Like Daylight
portfolio.excerpt: Launch copy and brand narrative for a wellness startup's flagship release.
portfolio.content:
Lumen didn't want to sound like another wellness brand promising transformation by Tuesday. They wanted daylight — clarity, warmth, the feeling of a window opening.

So we built the voice around a single idea: less noise, more light. Every headline earned its place. Every CTA felt like an invitation rather than a demand.

The launch sold through its first run in nine days. But the founder told me the thing she loved most was that, for the first time, the website sounded like her.

portfolio.title: A Field Manual for API Documentation
portfolio.excerpt: How we rebuilt a developer portal so engineers stopped emailing support.
portfolio.content:
Documentation is a product, and most companies ship it like an afterthought. Northstack was losing engineers to confusion in the first ten minutes.

We started with the questions real developers asked in Slack and turned them into a structured, searchable portal. We wrote for the person who is tired, on a deadline, and has zero interest in your brand story.

Support tickets dropped by a third. The engineering team stopped being the help desk. Good technical writing is, at its core, a kindness.

portfolio.title: Letters to a City That Raised Me
portfolio.excerpt: A series of love letters to Butwal — its markets, its monsoons, its unremarkable magic.
portfolio.content:
You won't find us on a postcard. But there is a particular gold to the light here in late summer, and the tea stalls on the corner have memorised my order.

I write these letters because cities like mine are rarely written about with tenderness. They are skipped over, assumed small. But small is not the same as insignificant.

So this is for the monsoons, and the markets, and the unremarkable magic of a place that raised a writer without ever meaning to.

portfolio.title: The Art of the Unsent Email
portfolio.excerpt: On the letters we write to no one, and what they teach us about the ones we do send.
portfolio.content:
I keep a folder of unsent emails. Messages to former bosses, to a version of myself, to people who will never read them. They are not drafts. They are exorcisms.

The strange thing is how much they sharpen the emails I do send. Once the anger is spent on the page no one sees, what remains is clearer, kinder, and far more useful.

Write the unsent one. Then delete it. Then write the real one in half the words.

blog.title: Why Your Brand Voice Is a Promise, Not a Style Guide
blog.excerpt: Voice isn't the fonts you pick. It's what the reader trusts you to deliver every single time.
blog.content:
Most brand guidelines I'm handed read like a costume. 'We are friendly but professional. We use contractions. We avoid jargon.' Useful, perhaps, but a costume is something you put on.

A real voice is a promise. It tells the reader: you can expect me to show up the same way, whether it's a tweet or a terms-of-service page. Trust is built in that consistency, not in the cleverness of any single line.

If you want a voice that lasts, stop dressing up. Start deciding what you will never say, and what you will always mean. The rest is just vocabulary.

blog.title: The 200-Word Rule for Killing Bad Openings
blog.excerpt: If your first 200 words don't earn the next 200, they shouldn't survive the edit.
blog.content:
I judge every draft by its first 200 words. Not for grammar — for permission. Does this paragraph earn the reader's attention for 200 more?

Most openings fail because they warm up. They clear their throat. They tell you what they're about to tell you. Readers don't need the throat-clearing; they need the stakes.

Cut the warm-up. Start where it matters. The 200-word rule is ruthless, and it will make you a faster, braver writer.

blog.title: Writing for Humans in the Age of Generated Text
blog.excerpt: When anyone can produce a thousand words, the scarce thing becomes a point of view.
blog.content:
The flood of generated text has changed the job. It is no longer impressive to produce words. It is impressive to have somewhere worth going with them.

Machines are fluent. They are not opinionated. The scarce commodity in 2025 is not content — it's a point of view, an argument, a felt sense of what matters.

So I've stopped competing on volume. I compete on conviction. That's the one thing the models can imitate but never own.

blog.title: A Writer's Case for Doing Nothing
blog.excerpt: The blank page fears busyness more than it fears silence. Here's why rest is part of the work.
blog.content:
We romanticise the tortured writer, but the truth is quieter: the best ideas arrive when you've stopped chasing them. Rest is not the opposite of work. It's the soil.

I keep a 'nothing' hour most afternoons. No notes, no notifications. Just a window and a thought allowed to wander. Half my good sentences show up there, uninvited.

If you're stuck, don't push harder. Step away. The page will still be there, and you'll finally have something to say to it.
